agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"A feature request proposes adding a 'confirmed' status to the Quote lifecycle. The term 'confirmed' is already established in the Order bounded context with a precise, well-defined meaning: paid and immutable. Reusing the same term in the Quote context with a fundamentally different meaning (verbal agreement, unpaid, mutable) creates a dangerous domain language collision.","bounded_context":"Quote Management vs Order Fulfillment — two distinct bounded contexts that share customer-facing language and handoff points. The Quote-to-Order transition is a critical domain boundary.","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Confirmed Order","definition":"An order that has been paid and is queued for fulfillment. It is immutable — no further changes are permitted. This is an established invariant."},{"term":"Confirmed Quote (proposed)","definition":"A quote where the customer has verbally agreed to terms but payment has NOT occurred. It is NOT immutable — terms could still change before payment. This is the new proposal."},{"term":"Quote","definition":"A priced proposal to a customer that may be accepted, rejected, or expire. It precedes an Order."},{"term":"Verbal Agreement","definition":"A non-binding customer indication of intent to proceed. Does not constitute payment or contractual commitment in most logistics contexts."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"The term 'confirmed' applied to an Order means paid and immutable. This definition is established and in active use.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Confirmed Order immutability is a data integrity invariant. If violated, fulfillment could proceed on stale or inconsistent data. Downstream systems (warehouse, dispatch, billing) depend on this guarantee.","testable_assertion":"No field on a Confirmed Order can be modified after it enters 'confirmed' status."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"A verbal agreement does not constitute payment. A quote with verbal agreement is not equivalent to a confirmed order.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Conflating verbal agreement with payment breaks financial integrity. An unpaid quote must never trigger fulfillment workflows.","testable_assertion":"A quote in any pre-payment status must not be processable by the fulfillment pipeline."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"Domain terms must have exactly one meaning within the system's shared language. If the same term means different things in different contexts, it must be explicitly disambiguated or a different term must be used.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is a modeling best practice, not a physical constraint — but violating it causes cascading misunderstandings across teams, code, and customer communications.","testable_assertion":"No single status label maps to two different semantic meanings across entity types that participate in the same business flow (Quote → Order)."},{"id":"BR-4","rule":"A quote with verbal customer agreement is still mutable — terms, pricing, and scope can change before payment.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Until payment occurs, the commercial terms are not locked. Sales agents routinely adjust quotes after verbal agreements. Making a verbally-agreed quote immutable would break the sales workflow.","testable_assertion":"A quote in 'verbally agreed' (or equivalent) status can still have its line items, pricing, and terms modified."}],"acceptance_criteria":[],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"A developer writes code that checks `status == 'confirmed'` without qualifying the entity type, affecting both Orders and Quotes.","expected_behavior":"The system must never allow a generic 'confirmed' check to conflate paid-immutable Orders with unpaid-mutable Quotes. This is the primary risk of the naming collision."},{"scenario":"A sales agent tells a customer their quote is 'confirmed' and the customer assumes it means the same as a 'confirmed order' — that fulfillment has begun.","expected_behavior":"Customer-facing language must clearly distinguish between 'quote accepted/agreed' and 'order confirmed'. A verbal agreement on a quote should never signal fulfillment initiation."},{"scenario":"A Quote transitions to 'confirmed' and then the customer changes their mind before paying.","expected_behavior":"The quote must be modifiable or cancellable. Unlike a confirmed Order, no irreversible downstream processes should have been triggered."},{"scenario":"Reporting dashboards or analytics that filter on 'confirmed' status now return a mixed set of paid orders and unpaid quotes.","expected_behavior":"Reports must not conflate these two populations. Revenue reporting on 'confirmed' items would be materially wrong if unpaid quotes are included."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"HIGH RISK. If 'confirmed' quotes are mistakenly treated as 'confirmed' orders anywhere in the system — reporting, revenue recognition, fulfillment triggering — the company could recognize revenue prematurely, ship goods without payment, or misstate financial position.","operational":"Significant confusion risk for operations teams, developers, and support staff. Every person and system that currently understands 'confirmed = paid and locked' must now ask 'confirmed what?' This increases error rates and slows decision-making.","user":"Sales agents get a workflow improvement (tracking verbal agreements), but customers face confusion risk if 'confirmed' language leaks into communications. A customer told their quote is 'confirmed' may expect delivery.","risk":"Domain language corruption is one of the highest-leverage risks in software systems. It doesn't cause a single bug — it causes a class of bugs that recur indefinitely. The Quote→Order handoff is the most critical boundary in the sales-to-fulfillment pipeline.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Sales Agents","impact":"Positive — they get a status to track verbal agreements. But terminology must be clear to avoid them promising 'confirmation' that implies fulfillment."},{"group":"Finance / Accounting","impact":"Negative — any reporting or revenue logic keyed on 'confirmed' status is now ambiguous. Risk of premature revenue recognition."},{"group":"Operations / Warehouse","impact":"Negative — if a 'confirmed' quote leaks into fulfillment queues, goods ship without payment."},{"group":"Engineering","impact":"Negative — every existing code path referencing 'confirmed' must be audited. New developers will consistently misunderstand the dual meaning."},{"group":"Customers","impact":"Mixed — verbal agreement tracking could speed up their experience, but 'confirmed' language in communications could set false expectations."}]},"confidence":{"level":40,"reasoning":"The spec contains a direct contradiction in domain language: 'confirmed' is defined as paid+immutable for Orders, but the feature request redefines it as unpaid+mutable for Quotes. These are not reconcilable under the same term. Acceptance criteria cannot be written until this fundamental naming collision is resolved.","high_confidence_areas":["The existing 'confirmed order' invariant is clear and well-established","Verbal agreement ≠ payment is a hard business fact","The naming collision is real and will cause concrete bugs"],"low_confidence_areas":["What the correct alternative term should be (requires stakeholder input)","Whether the Quote bounded context already has status transitions that constrain options","Whether there are regulatory implications for the term 'confirmed' in customer-facing contexts"],"assumptions":["The existing 'confirmed order = paid + immutable' definition is authoritative and non-negotiable","Quotes and Orders participate in the same business flow and share reporting/analytics infrastructure","The system does not already have robust entity-type-qualified status handling everywhere"]},"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"The term 'confirmed' is already defined as 'paid and immutable' for Orders. The feature request redefines 'confirmed' for Quotes to mean 'verbally agreed, unpaid, and mutable.' These are contradictory semantics for the same term within a shared business domain. Using the same word for opposite invariants (immutable vs mutable, paid vs unpaid) will corrupt the ubiquitous language.","affected_stakeholders":["Engineering","Finance","Operations","Sales","Customers"],"options":["A) Rename the Quote status to a distinct term: 'accepted', 'agreed', 'customer-approved', or 'verbally-committed' — preserving the existing meaning of 'confirmed' for Orders only","B) Rename the Order status away from 'confirmed' (e.g., 'paid', 'locked') and give 'confirmed' to Quotes — massive migration cost, not recommended","C) Qualify both: 'quote-confirmed' and 'order-confirmed' — reduces but does not eliminate confusion"],"recommendation":"Option A. The Quote status should use a different term — 'accepted' or 'customer-approved' are the strongest candidates. 'Confirmed' must remain reserved for its established meaning: paid and immutable. The new Quote status should explicitly encode that it represents a verbal, non-binding agreement. This preserves domain integrity at zero migration cost."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"Reuse of the term 'confirmed' with contradictory semantics across Quote and Order entities in the same business domain.","business_rule_broken":"BR-1 (confirmed = paid + immutable) and BR-3 (one term, one meaning). A 'confirmed' quote that is unpaid and mutable directly contradicts the established invariant that 'confirmed' means paid and immutable."}]}
